text: The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks, including the left costal margin and midclavicular line, while palpating the abdomen. The Palmer's point and Misra's point are marked, and the abdominal wall is held to reveal the boundary of the hernia and rectus diastasis.

The surgeon makes a small stab wound and stretches the abdominal wall downward to position the entry point below the costal margin. A Veress needle is inserted perpendicularly towards the stomach. The irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests are performed. The preset pressure is 15 mmHg, and the actual pressure is 8-9 mmHg. After inflating with one liter of gas, the flow rate increases, and once the actual pressure reaches the preset pressure, the Veress needle is removed, and the incision is enlarged.

The surgeon introduces a 10mm optical trocar and inserts the telescope to examine the hernia area. The baseball diamond concept is applied for port placement, with the telescope positioned 24 centimeters from the target and instruments 18 centimeters away. Three ports are planned for the abdominal wall repair.
The surgeon positions the right and left hand ports for the laparoscopic procedure. Upon entering the abdominal cavity, a large incarceration and extensive adhesions are visible.

The surgeon begins adhesiolysis using a left-hand atraumatic grasper to stretch the abdominal wall. The peritoneal dissection starts at the periphery of the bowel, avoiding direct contact with the bowel using the harmonic device. The vibrating jaw of the harmonic is used to dissect the boundaries of the peritoneum around the defect, which is completely incarcerated in the abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs meticulous dissection using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques to separate the bowel loops, keeping close to the abdominal wall to avoid recurrence, incarceration, and adhesion.
The surgeon observes the periphery between the medial structures and ligament, noting that the entire bowel is adhered to the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully carries out dissection, using the left hand to stretch the bowel loop downward while the right hand separates the sac peritoneum. The hernia sac is being meticulously separated, revealing pre-peritoneal fat and a clear plane for separation. Multiple defects are visible, and all defects are treated as a single defect for mesh sizing.

The surgeon performs aggressive dissection to address lateral adhesions with incarcerated bowel loops. The sac and peritoneum are carefully separated from the bowel loop, relying on visual interpretation due to limited tactile feedback in laparoscopy.
The surgeon identifies and delineates the boundaries of the bowel loops behind the hernia sac, preparing for further dissection to separate the adhered loops from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully holds the minimum amount of bowel and focuses on grasping the hernia sac to separate it from the surrounding tissue, proceeding with caution to avoid losing precision.

The surgeon identifies another defect in the hernia repair area, with the hernia sac visible and prepared for trimming. The previous roll-in suture is observed, but no mesh is found in this area, indicating that mesh was likely not used in the prior repair.
The surgeon trims the sac above the hernia defect to prevent traction on the bowel loop, ensuring careful separation of the sac from surrounding tissues.

Approximately seventy-five percent of the affected region has been removed, revealing a diastasis of the rectus muscles. The surgeon prepares to suture and close the rectus edges, as placing a mesh alone is insufficient for closure in this case.

The surgeon uses the left hand to stretch the bowel downward while the right hand operates a vibrating jaw instrument over the ring of the rectus muscle, cutting through fibrotic tissue to release the bowel without causing harm.
The surgeon completes the separation of the bowel loop in the surgical field, taking care to avoid the bladder area located below. The procedure is performed between the medial umbilical ligaments.

The surgeon inspects the bowel for any perforations, confirming its integrity. The defect is closed using an extracorporeal Mishra's knot technique with a number one Prolene suture. The rectus margins are approximated tightly, with multiple knots securing the closure. The pressure is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury to ensure proper approximation of the rectus edges.

The surgeon demonstrates the extracorporeal knot tying technique, showing the sequential process of creating the first hitch, wind, and lock, followed by the second and third wind and lock. The knot effectively pulls the edges of the rectus muscle closer together, with pressure reduced to optimize the approximation.

The surgeon carefully takes reverse bites along the margins to approximate the edges without leaving any defect. Multiple extracorporeal knots are used to close the defect, ensuring tight approximation of the tissue.

The surgeon applies Western knots to close intermediate defects, releasing tension and ensuring all defects are closed. The medial umbilical ligaments and bladder are visible between them. A polyurethane mesh with Gore-Tex sutures is positioned for trans-facial fixation, with sutures pulled out to stabilize the mesh corners. A titanium tacker is used for outer and inner crowning of the mesh, taking care to avoid the inferior epigastric vessels. Slight oozing from the hernia sac is noted, but pressure from the mesh is expected to stop it. The left hand guides the tacker placement by feeling the indentation. Additional tackers are used to release tension and ensure better fixation. Finally, strapping with a sponge and elastic adhesive is applied for abdominal support to prevent seroma formation.
